section.title: The Guilt Offering
section.sectionRationale: Closing the sacrificial law collection's fifth offering, this passage first lists specific cases requiring a sin offering, scaled for the poor and poorest, then introduces the guilt offering, which uniquely requires restitution, repaying what was wrongly taken plus a fifth, alongside the sacrifice.
section.thesis: God provides atonement scaled to what a sinner can afford, from a lamb down to a handful of flour, for specific unintentional sins, and where sin has actually damaged another person or defrauded God's holy things, He requires not only sacrifice but full restitution plus a fifth, showing that true repentance repairs as well as atones.
section.frameNote: Four movements: specific cases requiring a sin offering; the sliding scale of provision for the poor; the guilt offering for sins against holy things; and the guilt offering for sins of deception and fraud against a neighbor, requiring restitution.

verses.text: “If someone sins by failing to testify when he hears a public charge about something he has witnessed, whether he has seen it or learned of it, he shall bear the iniquity.
Or if a person touches anything {{tame|unclean}}, whether the carcass of any unclean wild animal or livestock or crawling creature, even if he is unaware of it, he is unclean and guilty.
Or if he touches human uncleanness, anything by which one becomes unclean, even if he is unaware of it, when he realizes it, he is guilty.

Or if someone swears thoughtlessly with his lips to do anything good or evil, in whatever matter a man may rashly pronounce an oath, even if he is unaware of it, when he realizes it, he is guilty in the matter.
If someone incurs guilt in one of these ways, he must confess the sin he has committed,

units.label: Scaled to what the sinner can afford
units.note: a lamb, two birds, or fine flour

verses.text: and he must bring his {{asham|guilt offering}} to the LORD for the sin he has committed: a female lamb or goat from the flock as a sin offering. And the priest will make atonement for him concerning his sin.
If, however, he cannot afford a lamb, he may bring to the LORD as restitution for his sin two turtledoves or two young pigeons, one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering.
He is to bring them to the priest, who shall first present the one for the sin offering. He is to twist its head at the front of its neck without severing it;
then he is to sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar, while the rest of the blood is drained out at the base of the altar. It is a sin offering.
And the priest must prepare the second bird as a burnt offering according to the ordinance. In this way the priest will make atonement for him for the sin he has committed, and he will be forgiven.

But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two young pigeons, he may bring a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a sin offering. He must not put olive oil or frankincense on it, because it is a sin offering.
He is to bring it to the priest, who shall take a handful from it as a memorial portion and burn it on the altar atop the offerings made by fire to the LORD; it is a sin offering.
In this way the priest will make atonement for him for any of these sins he has committed, and he will be forgiven. The remainder will belong to the priest, like the grain offering.”

units.label: Guilt against the LORD's holy things

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,

“If someone acts unfaithfully and sins unintentionally against any of the LORD's holy things, he must bring his guilt offering to the LORD: an unblemished ram from the flock, of proper value in silver shekels according to the sanctuary shekel; it is a guilt offering.

Regarding any holy thing he has harmed, he must make restitution by adding a fifth of its value to it and giving it to the priest, who will make atonement on his behalf with the ram as a guilt offering, and he will be forgiven.
If someone sins and violates any of the LORD's commandments even though he was unaware, he is guilty and shall bear his punishment.

He is to bring to the priest an unblemished ram of proper value from the flock as a guilt offering. Then the priest will make atonement on his behalf for the wrong he has committed in ignorance, and he will be forgiven.
It is a guilt offering; he was certainly guilty before the LORD.”
And the LORD said to Moses,
“If someone sins and acts unfaithfully against the LORD by deceiving his neighbor in regard to a deposit or security entrusted to him or stolen, or if he extorts his neighbor
or finds lost property and lies about it and swears falsely, or if he commits any such sin that a man might commit,
once he has sinned and becomes guilty, he must return what he has stolen or taken by extortion, or the deposit entrusted to him, or the lost property he found,
or anything else about which he has sworn falsely. He must make restitution in full, add a fifth of the value, and pay it to the owner on the day he acknowledges his guilt.
Then he must bring to the priest his guilt offering to the LORD: an unblemished ram of proper value from the flock.
In this way the priest will make atonement for him before the LORD, and he will be forgiven for anything he may have done to incur guilt.”

tame.gloss: Unclean, ceremonially defiled. Contact with certain carcasses or human uncleanness rendered a person tame even without any conscious awareness, showing that defilement, like sin, can be real regardless of whether a person notices it happening.

asham.gloss: The guilt offering, distinct from the sin offering, chattat, in that it specifically addresses sins that have caused measurable harm or defrauded holy things or a neighbor, requiring restitution in addition to sacrifice.

basic.context: This passage closes the sacrificial law's first collection. It first lists specific cases, failing to testify, touching something unclean, or a rash oath, that require a sin offering, scaled generously down to a handful of flour for the very poor. Then it introduces the guilt offering, required whenever sin has caused actual damage, either to God's holy things or to a neighbor's property, and this offering always comes with restitution, repaying what was taken plus a fifth.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes Israel's actual old covenant provisions for atonement and restitution; it is not a legal code for Christians to reenact today. Its abiding truth is that God's mercy scales down to whatever the poorest sinner can bring, and that genuine repentance includes repairing the actual harm done, not sacrifice alone.
basic.originalLanguages: Even unnoticed contact with something tame, unclean, created real guilt, showing that defilement does not depend on awareness. The asham, guilt offering, is distinguished from the ordinary chattat, sin offering, because it specifically addresses damage done, whether against God's holy things or a neighbor's property, and always requires restitution alongside the sacrifice.
basic.typology: The sliding scale, lamb, then birds, then flour, pictures a God who meets sinners exactly where their means allow, anticipating a gospel with no economic barrier to entry. The requirement of restitution plus a fifth foreshadows the New Testament pattern of repentance that includes making things right, as seen in Zacchaeus's fourfold repayment, Luke 19:8.
basic.christ: Christ is the guilt offering, asham, that Isaiah 53:10 foretells, the Servant whose life is made an offering for guilt, so that our account with God is not only forgiven but genuinely settled, restitution paid in full by Him on our behalf.
basic.god: God's justice requires that actual harm be repaired, not merely apologized for, yet His mercy provides an offering within reach of every sinner regardless of wealth. He is neither an indifferent judge who overlooks real damage nor a God whose provision for atonement excludes the poor.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:10 calls the Suffering Servant's life a guilt offering, asham, made for our sin. Luke 19:8 shows Zacchaeus's repentance including fourfold restitution, echoing this pattern of repair alongside confession. 1 John 1:9 promises forgiveness and cleansing to those who confess.
basic.oneStory: This passage shows a God who provides atonement scaled to what every sinner can afford and who requires that real harm be genuinely repaired, not merely forgiven in word. It anticipates Christ, our guilt offering, whose sacrifice both forgives our sin and settles our account with God completely.

context.literary: This section closes the opening block of sacrificial law that began in chapter one, moving from the general sacrifices, burnt, grain, and peace, through the sin offering, to this final category, the guilt offering, marked by its unique requirement of restitution.
context.historical: Ancient Israel's economy depended on trust between neighbors, deposits, loans, and found property; this law gave a concrete process for repairing broken trust rather than leaving wronged parties without recourse.
context.canonical: The guilt offering, asham, becomes the specific term Isaiah uses to describe the Suffering Servant's death in Isaiah 53:10, making this otherwise technical chapter directly foundational to one of the most explicit Old Testament prophecies of Christ's atoning death.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide atonement accessible to every economic level for specific unintentional sins, and to establish that sins causing actual damage, to holy things or to a neighbor, require both sacrificial atonement and full restitution plus a fifth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes actual legal and sacrificial procedures for old covenant Israel; it is not a civil code binding on Christians today. Its abiding principle is that true repentance includes repairing real harm done to others, not sacrifice or apology alone.

debates.question: Why does restitution require repaying the value plus an additional fifth, rather than the exact amount taken?

views.label: A penalty that discourages theft and deception
views.case: The added fifth functions as a penalty beyond simple repayment, making dishonesty costly rather than merely neutral once discovered.

views.label: Compensation for loss of use and trust
views.case: Others see the added fifth as compensating the wronged party for the time their property was withheld and the trust that was broken, beyond the item's bare value.

debates.bearing: Either way, the added fifth teaches that repentance for wrongs against others involves more than returning to a neutral position; it requires generous repair.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter distinguishes tame, unclean, defilement that is real even when unnoticed, from asham, the guilt offering specifically required when sin has caused measurable damage, either to God's qodesh, holy things, or to a neighbor. That damage always required restitution alongside the sacrifice, a pattern the prophet Isaiah later applies directly to the Messiah's atoning death.

typology.type: The sliding scale of provision, lamb, birds, or flour
typology.fulfillment: A gospel accessible to every sinner regardless of what they can bring, 2 Corinthians 8:9.

typology.type: Restitution plus a fifth for damage done
typology.fulfillment: Genuine repentance that repairs real harm, as seen in Zacchaeus's fourfold restitution, Luke 19:8, and ultimately in Christ's guilt offering that fully settles our account with God, Isaiah 53:10.

study.christ: Isaiah 53:10 explicitly names the Suffering Servant's death an asham, a guilt offering, made for our sin. Where Israel's guilt offering required the sinner himself to make restitution alongside the sacrifice, Christ both bears the guilt offering and, in His resurrection life, secures the full and final restitution of everything sin had damaged, reconciling all things to God, Colossians 1:20.
study.god: God's justice will not let real damage go unaddressed, requiring restitution plus a fifth wherever sin has caused actual harm, yet His mercy meets every sinner at the level of their means, scaling the offering down to flour for the very poor. He is a God who takes both the reality of harm and the reality of poverty with equal seriousness.

crossReferences.note: The Suffering Servant's life is made an asham, a guilt offering, for the sin of His people.
Zacchaeus's repentance includes fourfold restitution, echoing the pattern of repair alongside confession established here.
God is faithful and just to forgive our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness when we confess.

study.oneStory: This closing section of the sacrificial law shows a God who provides atonement within reach of every sinner, however poor, and who requires that real damage done to others be genuinely repaired, not merely forgiven in word. It anticipates Christ, the true guilt offering, whose death both forgives sinners and fully settles what sin had broken.

quiz.q: What provision does God make for someone too poor to afford even two turtledoves or two young pigeons for the sin offering?

options.text: A tenth of an ephah of fine flour, with no oil or frankincense.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13 describe this final, most accessible level of provision.

options.text: He is exempt from bringing any offering.
options.note: The text scales the offering down rather than exempting anyone from bringing it.

options.text: He must wait until he can afford a lamb.
options.note: The provision allows immediate atonement at whatever level the person can afford, not a delay.

options.text: No further provision is made below the level of birds.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13 explicitly provide flour as a further accessible option.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the guilt offering, asham, from the ordinary sin offering in this passage?

options.text: It requires restitution, repaying the value plus a fifth, in addition to the sacrifice.
options.note: Verses 16 and 6:5 both specify this added restitution as unique to cases requiring the guilt offering.

options.text: It requires a more expensive animal in every case without exception.
options.note: While a ram is specified, the defining and unique feature is the restitution requirement, not merely the cost of the animal.

options.text: It can only be offered by priests.
options.note: The guilt offering is brought by the individual who sinned, not offered exclusively by priests.

options.text: There is no difference between the two offerings.
options.note: The restitution requirement clearly distinguishes the guilt offering from the sin offering.

quiz.q: What must someone do who has defrauded a neighbor through a false oath about lost property, according to Leviticus 6:1-7?

options.text: Return the property in full, add a fifth of its value, and bring a guilt offering to the priest.
options.note: Verses 4 to 6 describe exactly this combination of restitution and sacrifice.

options.text: Simply confess the sin with no further action required.
options.note: The passage requires both restitution and sacrifice, not confession alone.

options.text: Pay double the value of the property to the priest.
options.note: The text specifies a fifth added to the full value, paid to the wronged owner, not double payment to the priest.

options.text: No remedy is described for this situation.
options.note: Verses 1 to 7 give a detailed remedy for this exact situation.

quiz.q: What examples of guilt does Leviticus 5:1-4 list as requiring a sin offering?

options.text: Failing to testify as a witness, touching something unclean unknowingly, and swearing a rash oath.
options.note: Verses 1 to 4 list exactly these three categories of unintentional guilt.

options.text: Only deliberate murder and theft.
options.note: Those are serious deliberate sins addressed elsewhere; this passage concerns unintentional guilt.

options.text: Failing to pay the temple tax.
options.note: This is not among the cases listed in verses 1 to 4.

options.text: No specific examples are given.
options.note: Verses 1 to 4 give specific, concrete examples.

quiz.q: According to Isaiah 53:10, what does the prophet call the Suffering Servant's life given for sin?

options.text: A guilt offering, asham, the same term used throughout this Leviticus passage.
options.note: Isaiah 53:10 uses this specific sacrificial term to describe the Servant's atoning death.

options.text: A burnt offering only.
options.note: Isaiah's specific term is guilt offering, asham, not burnt offering, olah.

options.text: A peace offering.
options.note: Isaiah's term is specifically the guilt offering, not the peace offering.

options.text: No sacrificial term is used.
options.note: Isaiah 53:10 explicitly uses sacrificial language, asham, to describe the Servant's death.

questions: God scaled the offering down to whatever the poorest person could afford, even a handful of flour. Do you ever feel you cannot approach God because you have too little to offer Him?
Some guilt in this passage came from things done in complete unawareness, touching something unclean without realizing it. Where might you be guilty of something you have not yet even recognized?

The guilt offering required not just sacrifice but restitution, actually repairing the damage done. Is there a person you have wronged whom you need to actually repay or make right with, not just apologize to?
Restitution required paying back the full value plus a fifth, more than simply breaking even. Where might God be calling you to generosity in making amends, beyond the bare minimum?

Christ is called our guilt offering in Isaiah 53:10, His life given to settle our account with God completely. Do you live as though your account with God is truly settled, or do you still feel you owe Him something Christ has already paid?

translationNotes.note: Even if he is unaware of it shows that ceremonial and moral guilt in this system did not depend on conscious knowledge; unintentional contact still required atonement, underscoring the objective seriousness of sin and defilement.
The LORD's holy things likely refers to sacred offerings, tithes, or dedicated property; sinning against them, even unintentionally, required both restitution to the sanctuary and a guilt offering.

Add a fifth of the value reflects a consistent restitution formula appearing also in verse 16 and elsewhere in the law, compare Leviticus 27:13, establishing a standard penalty for wrongful use of another's property.
